metadata.title: 401(k) Loan Basics and Support Guide
metadata.description: Global AI support guide for 401(k) loan requests covering participant and plan eligibility checks, record-keeper-based routing, MFA-dependent online access considerations, general loan availability rules, vested-balance-based loan limits, repayment terms, primary residence documentation, loan status review, missing-funds troubleshooting, repayment increase rules, full payoff handling, provider transitions, employment and plan termination impacts, loan offset tax treatment, rollover timing, record-keeper-dependent fee-disclosure rules for Vanguard and LT Trust plans, and support-routing requirements when plan-specific or prior 12-month loan-history details cannot be confirmed.

rules: Not every retirement plan offers loans, so eligibility depends on the employer's plan rules.
In general, a participant must be actively employed, have enough vested balance available, and meet IRS loan requirements.
Plans may set their own minimum vested balance and may limit how many loans a participant can have at once.
The maximum amount a participant can generally borrow is 50% of the vested account balance, up to $50,000.
The $50,000 cap is reduced by the highest outstanding loan balance the participant had during the previous 12 months, even if that earlier loan has already been repaid.
A plan may require a minimum loan amount, such as $1,000.
Most 401(k) loans are repaid automatically through payroll deductions.
The standard repayment term is usually up to 5 years.
If the loan is used to purchase a primary residence, the repayment term may extend up to 15 years, and supporting documentation is required.
Participants who want to repay faster may be able to increase payroll deductions only in exact multiples of the original scheduled payment.
The repayment schedule is not reamortized when higher payroll deductions are used.
Participants cannot choose an arbitrary increased payment amount.
A participant may pay off the loan early by increasing payroll deductions or paying the entire remaining balance in one full payoff.
Partial external payments by check, ACH, or wire are not accepted unless they cover the full remaining balance.
There is no prepayment penalty for paying the loan off in full.
Loan proceeds may appear as a withdrawal, check, wire, or ACH transfer.
If funds were sent by check and more than two weeks have passed since the check was issued, a stop payment and reissue may be requested.
If a replacement check is being switched to ACH, updated authorization forms can be sent by support.
If the original delivery method was wire or ACH, the transfer is typically processed by Mid Atlantic Trust Company.
If a wire or ACH fails because of incorrect banking details, name mismatch, or account type issues, the funds are automatically reissued by check to the address on file.
When funds are automatically reissued by check after a failed wire or ACH, the participant is asked to wait two full weeks from the check issue date before requesting another reissue.
If an employer changes 401(k) providers, an outstanding loan will usually transfer with the participant's account as long as the participant is still actively employed.
The remaining balance and repayment schedule generally stay the same, and payroll deductions continue after the transition is complete.
Participants do not usually need to reapply for the loan or restart repayment.
There may be a short blackout period during the provider change when account access and loan activity are temporarily unavailable.
After the transition, there may be a brief delay before the loan appears on the new provider's dashboard.
If employment ends while a loan is still outstanding, the participant will typically have about 90 days to repay the remaining balance.
If the balance is not repaid in time after employment ends, it is generally treated as a loan offset or deemed distribution and becomes taxable income.
The amount will be reported on Form 1099-R, and if the participant is under age 59 1/2, a 10% early withdrawal penalty may also apply.

A participant may avoid taxes and penalties after a loan offset by completing a rollover of an amount equal to the offset into an IRA or another eligible retirement plan by the federal tax return due date for the year of the offset, including extensions.
If the employer terminates the 401(k) plan, payroll loan repayments stop, and the remaining balance is generally due in full before the plan closes.
If the loan is not repaid before plan closure, it is treated as a taxable distribution and may be subject to taxes and penalties.
Once the plan is fully terminated and distributed, online account access is disabled.
Record Keeper must be identified first before disclosing any loan-related fee.
If the Record Keeper is RK: Vanguard, disclose the Vanguard fee rules only.
If the Record Keeper is RK: LT Trust, disclose the LT Trust fee rules only.
If the Record Keeper is neither RK: Vanguard nor RK: LT Trust, support must confirm any loan-related fees before they are disclosed.

fees.notes: Applies only to RK: Vanguard plans. The fee is taken out of the loan proceeds. Record Keeper must be identified first before disclosing any loan-related fee. If Record Keeper cannot be determined, redirect to support.

Applies only to RK: LT Trust plans. Deducted from participant account balance. Record Keeper must be identified first before disclosing any loan-related fee. If Record Keeper cannot be determined, redirect to support.

Annual fee. Applies only to RK: LT Trust plans. Deducted from participant account balance. Record Keeper must be identified first before disclosing any loan-related fee. If Record Keeper cannot be determined, redirect to support.

fees.service: LT Trust regular mail check fee
fees.notes: No additional charges apply for a Regular Mail Check for LT Trust plans. Record Keeper must be identified first before disclosing any loan-related fee. If Record Keeper cannot be determined, redirect to support.

Additional fee if wire is selected as the disbursal payment method when requesting a loan. Applies only to RK: LT Trust plans. This fee applies per wire, not per request, and is non-refundable. Record Keeper must be identified first before disclosing any loan-related fee. If Record Keeper cannot be determined, redirect to support.

Additional fee if Overnight Check is selected as the disbursal payment method when requesting a loan. Applies only to RK: LT Trust plans. This fee applies per overnight check, not per request, and is non-refundable. Record Keeper must be identified first before disclosing any loan-related fee. If Record Keeper cannot be determined, redirect to support.

steps.description: Check the participant's status in the profile.
steps.notes: Confirm whether the profile's Eligibility Status supports mapping the participant to an Active Participant Status for this workflow. Only active employees should be guided through the loan-request path described here.

steps.description: Check the participant's MFA status.
steps.notes: Confirm whether MFA status is enrolled. MFA status is only used to determine whether the LT Trust online account flow may be available.

steps.description: Check the participant's record keeper in the RK section.
steps.notes: The record keeper determines which article, login path, website guidance, and fee rules should be used. For RK: LT Trust, use the LT Trust article path. For RK: Vanguard, use the Vanguard article path.

steps.description: Check whether the participant's plan offers loans.
steps.notes: Review the Maximum Number of Loans section in the participant profile or other plan loan availability fields used internally. The participant can only request a loan if the plan offers loans.

steps.description: Check whether the participant already has active unpaid loan balances.
steps.notes: Review loan records and Outstanding Balance values to determine whether any current loans are still active and unpaid, because this may affect how many new loans the participant can request.

steps.description: After steps 1 through 5 are confirmed, guide the participant using the correct route.
steps.notes: If the record keeper is 'RK: LT Trust', follow the article 'LT: 401(k) Loan Complete Guide — Submission, Repayment & Support'. If the record keeper is 'RK: Vanguard', follow the article 'Vanguard: How to Submit a Loan Request'. If the record keeper is neither 'RK: LT Trust' nor 'RK: Vanguard', do not route to those record-keeper-specific articles. Instead, provide only the participant's confirmed general loan guidance from this article and direct the participant to support for record-keeper-specific help.

steps.description: If the participant asks about loan-related fees, confirm the Record Keeper before disclosing any fee details.
steps.notes: If the Record Keeper is RK: Vanguard, disclose the one-time $150 loan fee taken from proceeds. If the Record Keeper is RK: LT Trust, disclose only the LT Trust fee rules confirmed in scope. If the Record Keeper is neither, do not disclose or estimate a fee and redirect the participant to support for fee confirmation.

steps.description: If the participant is considering a primary residence loan, advise that supporting documentation is required, such as a signed purchase agreement or loan estimate.
steps.notes: The longer repayment term may extend up to 15 years for this purpose.

steps.description: If the participant submitted the wrong loan purpose, direct them to contact support so the request can be rejected and resubmitted correctly.
steps.notes: This applies when the participant accidentally chose the wrong loan purpose.

steps.description: After submission, have the participant check status in the plan dashboard under Loans, Distributions, or Transaction History.
steps.notes: Pending or completed activity may show the disbursement method and amount.

steps.description: If status is unclear, help verify the request and the expected delivery timeline.
steps.notes: Processing usually takes about 1 to 2 business days, but timing may vary by provider and delivery method.

steps.description: If funds were supposed to arrive by check and more than two weeks have passed since issue, explain that a stop payment and reissue may be requested.
steps.notes: The participant will need to confirm the correct mailing address.

steps.description: If the participant wants to switch from a replacement check to ACH, explain that support can send updated authorization forms.
steps.notes: This applies only when the participant is changing the replacement delivery method.

steps.description: If the original delivery method was wire or ACH and the transfer failed, explain that the funds are automatically reissued by check to the address on file.
steps.notes: Common failure reasons listed in the article are incorrect banking details, name mismatch, or account type issues.

steps.description: If funds were automatically reissued by check after a failed wire or ACH, tell the participant to wait two full weeks from the check issue date before requesting another reissue.
steps.notes: Do not shorten this waiting period.

steps.description: If the participant wants to repay faster, explain that payroll deductions may be increased only in exact multiples of the original scheduled payment.
steps.notes: The loan is not reamortized, so terms and interest calculation do not change.

steps.description: If the participant wants to pay off the loan early, explain that they may either increase payroll deductions or make one full payoff of the entire remaining balance.
steps.notes: Partial external payments are not accepted unless they cover the full remaining balance.

steps.description: If an employer changes providers, explain that the loan will usually transfer with the account if the participant is still actively employed.
steps.notes: There may be a short blackout period and a brief delay before the loan appears on the new provider's dashboard.

steps.description: If the participant leaves employment with an outstanding loan, explain that the remaining balance will typically need to be repaid within about 90 days.
steps.notes: If not repaid in time, the unpaid amount is generally treated as a taxable loan offset or deemed distribution.

steps.description: If a loan offset occurs after job separation, explain that the participant may avoid taxes and penalties by rolling over an equal amount into an IRA or another eligible retirement plan by the federal tax return due date for the year of the offset, including extensions.
steps.notes: The article provides an example with April 15, 2026 and October 15, 2026 for a 2025 offset.

steps.description: If the employer terminates the plan, explain that payroll repayments stop and the remaining balance is generally due in full before the plan closes.
steps.notes: Once the plan is fully terminated and distributed, online account access is disabled.

steps.description: Provide support contact options when the participant needs help with eligibility, payoff requests, repayment changes, missing funds, provider transitions, or tax-related loan questions.
steps.notes: The article lists help@forusall.com and 844-401-2253.

common_issues.issue: Participant wants the exact maximum loan amount, but prior 12-month highest outstanding loan balance cannot be verified.
common_issues.resolution: Explain the general rule that the maximum amount is typically 50% of the vested account balance up to $50,000, but that this cap may be reduced by the participant's highest outstanding loan balance during the previous 12 months. If that 12-month loan history is not available, do not confirm the exact maximum and refer the participant to human support.

common_issues.issue: Participant disputes the estimated maximum loan amount because they had a prior loan within the last 12 months.
common_issues.resolution: Do not override or recalculate the amount without verified 12-month outstanding loan history. Explain that prior loan history may reduce the cap and direct the participant to human support for confirmation.

common_issues.issue: Participant cannot tell whether the plan allows loans.
common_issues.resolution: Review internal profile checks first, including the Maximum Number of Loans section and other plan loan-availability fields. The participant may also review the Loans & Distributions section in the portal, but internal verification should come first.

common_issues.issue: Participant selected the wrong loan purpose.
common_issues.resolution: Advise the participant to contact support so the request can be rejected and resubmitted correctly.

common_issues.issue: Loan status is unclear after submission.
common_issues.resolution: Have the participant review Loans, Distributions, or Transaction History in the dashboard. If status is still unclear, help verify the request and expected delivery timeline.

common_issues.issue: Loan funds sent by check have not arrived.
common_issues.resolution: If more than two weeks have passed since the check was issued, explain that a stop payment and reissue may be requested and confirm the mailing address.

common_issues.issue: Wire or ACH transfer failed.
common_issues.resolution: Explain that failed transfers caused by incorrect banking details, name mismatch, or account type issues are automatically reissued by check to the address on file.

common_issues.issue: Participant wants to make an irregular larger payment.
common_issues.resolution: Explain that increased payroll deductions must be exact multiples of the original scheduled payment and that arbitrary amounts are not allowed.

common_issues.issue: Loan does not appear after a provider transition.
common_issues.resolution: Explain that there may be a brief delay after the transition. If the loan still does not appear after a few business days, the participant should contact the new provider or custodian, and ForUsAll can also help verify whether the loan transferred correctly.

common_issues.issue: Participant left employment and is unsure what happens to the outstanding loan.
common_issues.resolution: Explain that the participant will typically have about 90 days to repay the remaining balance and that failure to repay may result in a taxable loan offset or deemed distribution reported on Form 1099-R.

common_issues.issue: Participant is active but MFA is not enrolled, so the LT Trust online submission path is uncertain.
common_issues.resolution: Confirm the participant's MFA status internally before directing them to an LT Trust online loan path. If MFA is not enrolled, do not assume the participant can use the LT Trust online account flow.

common_issues.issue: Participant is not sure which record keeper applies to the plan.
common_issues.resolution: Check the RK section in the participant profile before sharing any login website or article. If the record keeper is RK: LT Trust, use the LT Trust article and ForUsAll login route. If the record keeper is RK: Vanguard, use the Vanguard article and Vanguard login route. If the record keeper is neither, provide only the general article guidance supported by the participant's confirmed profile data and route record-keeper-specific help to support.

common_issues.issue: Participant is asking for a loan path before participant status has been confirmed.
common_issues.resolution: Confirm internally whether the profile's Eligibility Status supports an Active Participant Status before guiding the participant through the loan-request route. Do not direct a participant through the standard loan-request path until the profile confirms the required status for that path.

common_issues.issue: Participant wants to request a loan, but the plan's loan availability has not been confirmed.
common_issues.resolution: Review the plan loan availability fields, including the Maximum Number of Loans section in the participant profile, before telling the participant how to proceed. Do not assume the plan offers loans.

common_issues.issue: Participant may already have an active unpaid loan and wants to know whether another loan can be requested.
common_issues.resolution: Review current loan records and Outstanding Balance values to determine whether any listed loans are still active and unpaid. Any Outstanding Balance greater than $0.00 should be treated as active or unpaid; $0.00 should be treated as not active or unpaid.

common_issues.issue: Participant wants to use a primary residence loan but has not provided supporting documentation.
common_issues.resolution: Explain that a longer repayment term for a primary residence loan may require supporting documentation, such as a signed purchase agreement or loan estimate. Do not confirm the longer term until that documentation requirement is satisfied.

common_issues.issue: Participant wants to know the maximum amount they can borrow.
common_issues.resolution: Explain the general rule that the maximum amount is typically 50% of the vested account balance, up to $50,000, but that the cap is reduced by the highest outstanding loan balance during the previous 12 months. Also note that plan-specific minimums or other limits may apply.

common_issues.issue: Participant had a prior loan in the last 12 months and does not understand why the new maximum is reduced.
common_issues.resolution: Explain that the general $50,000 cap is reduced by the highest outstanding loan balance the participant had during the previous 12 months, even if that prior loan has already been repaid. Use the article's example logic when clarifying the rule.

common_issues.issue: Participant wants to switch from a replacement check to ACH.
common_issues.resolution: Explain that if the participant wants to switch from a replacement check to ACH, support can send updated authorization forms. Do not promise the change will happen automatically without the required updated forms.

common_issues.issue: Participant wants to make a partial payoff by check, ACH, or wire.
common_issues.resolution: Explain that partial external payments are not accepted unless they cover the full remaining balance. If the participant wants to pay off the loan early, they may either increase payroll deductions in exact multiples or make one full payoff of the entire remaining balance.

common_issues.issue: Participant asks about loan fees before the Record Keeper is confirmed, or available workflow data is incomplete for fee disclosure.
common_issues.resolution: Explain that the Record Keeper must be identified first before any loan-related fee can be disclosed. Fees may vary by Record Keeper or custodian and should not be generalized across all plans. If the Record Keeper cannot be determined from available data or fee details cannot be safely confirmed, redirect the participant to support for fee confirmation.

common_issues.issue: Participant with RK: Vanguard asks about loan fees, including whether the fee is deducted separately or taken from the loan amount.
common_issues.resolution: If the Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: Vanguard, explain that there is a one-time loan fee of $150 and that it is taken out of the loan proceeds.

common_issues.issue: Participant with RK: LT Trust asks what fees apply, including delivery fee details and refundability.
common_issues.resolution: If the Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: LT Trust, disclose only the LT Trust fee rules that are confirmed in the article or workflow, including any applicable processing, maintenance, wire, overnight check, or regular mail check fee rules. Wire transfer and overnight check fees are non-refundable. No additional charges apply for a Regular Mail Check.

common_issues.issue: Participant asks about loan fees for a plan whose Record Keeper is neither RK: Vanguard nor RK: LT Trust.
common_issues.resolution: Explain that support must confirm any loan-related fees first for non-Vanguard and non-LT Trust plans. Do not disclose or estimate a fee before support confirmation.

common_issues.issue: Participant disputes a disclosed fee because they expected a different amount.
common_issues.resolution: Reconfirm the participant's Record Keeper and disclose only the fee rules that apply to that confirmed route. If the Record Keeper is not Vanguard or LT Trust, or if the fee rule cannot be confirmed, direct the participant to support.

examples.scenario: A participant asks about loan fees, but the Record Keeper has not yet been confirmed.
examples.outcome: Explain that the Record Keeper must be identified first before any loan-related fee can be disclosed. If the Record Keeper cannot be determined, redirect the participant to support for fee confirmation.

examples.scenario: A participant's Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: Vanguard and they ask whether a loan fee applies.
examples.outcome: Explain that for RK: Vanguard plans, there is a one-time loan fee of $150 and that it is taken out of the loan proceeds.

examples.scenario: A participant's Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: LT Trust and they ask what loan fees may apply.
examples.outcome: Explain only the LT Trust fee rules that are confirmed in the article or workflow, including any applicable processing, maintenance, wire, overnight check, or regular mail check fee rules.

examples.scenario: A participant's Record Keeper is neither RK: Vanguard nor RK: LT Trust and they ask for the exact loan processing fee.
examples.outcome: Do not disclose or estimate a fee. Explain that support must confirm any loan-related fees first for non-Vanguard and non-LT Trust plans.

examples.scenario: A participant asks whether the same loan processing fee applies to every plan.
examples.outcome: Explain that fees may vary by Record Keeper or custodian and should not be generalized across all plans.

examples.scenario: A participant with RK: Vanguard asks whether the loan fee is paid separately or comes out of the loan amount.
examples.outcome: Explain that for RK: Vanguard plans, the one-time $150 loan fee is taken out of the loan proceeds.

examples.scenario: A participant with RK: LT Trust asks whether Regular Mail Check has an extra delivery charge.
examples.outcome: Explain that for LT Trust plans, no additional charges apply for a Regular Mail Check, if that fee rule is confirmed in scope for the request.

examples.scenario: A participant with RK: LT Trust asks what happens if wire delivery is chosen for the loan disbursal.
examples.outcome: Explain that for LT Trust plans, an additional $35 wire fee applies if wire is selected, and that the fee applies per wire, not per request.

examples.scenario: A participant with RK: LT Trust asks what happens if Overnight Check is selected for loan delivery.
examples.outcome: Explain that for LT Trust plans, an additional $50 Overnight Check fee applies, and that the fee applies per overnight check, not per request.

examples.scenario: A participant with RK: LT Trust asks whether wire or Overnight Check fees can be refunded later.
examples.outcome: Explain that for LT Trust plans, wire transfer and Overnight Check fees are non-refundable.

examples.scenario: A participant asks for the exact maximum loan amount, but the available data does not show the highest outstanding loan balance during the previous 12 months.
examples.outcome: Explain the general loan limit rule, but do not confirm the exact maximum amount. Refer the participant to human support because the prior 12-month outstanding loan history may reduce the cap and cannot be fully verified.

examples.scenario: A participant says they had a loan within the last year, but the available system view does not confirm the highest outstanding balance during that period.
examples.outcome: Do not estimate or assume the reduction amount. Explain that the prior 12-month loan history may affect the maximum new loan amount and route the participant to human support for confirmation.

examples.scenario: A participant appears eligible based on active status, vested balance, and plan loan availability, but the exact borrowing limit may depend on prior 12-month loan history that is not visible.
examples.outcome: Provide only the general borrowing rule and explain that the final maximum amount requires human support review if the prior 12-month highest outstanding balance cannot be verified.

examples.scenario: A participant had a $10,000 outstanding loan at some point during the previous 12 months and is now requesting a new loan.
examples.outcome: Explain that the general $50,000 maximum new loan cap could be reduced to $40,000 because the cap is reduced by the highest outstanding loan balance during the previous 12 months.

examples.scenario: A participant wants to use a loan to purchase a primary residence.
examples.outcome: Explain that the repayment term may extend up to 15 years, but supporting documentation such as a signed purchase agreement or loan estimate is required.

examples.scenario: A participant's original scheduled payment is $91.09 per paycheck and they want to pay faster.
examples.outcome: Explain that they may choose exact multiples such as $182.18 or $273.27, but not an arbitrary amount like $100 or $110.

examples.scenario: A participant leaves a job in March 2025 with a $6,000 unpaid loan balance that becomes an offset.
examples.outcome: Explain that they may avoid taxation by rolling over $6,000 into an IRA or another eligible retirement plan by April 15, 2026, or October 15, 2026 if they file an extension.

examples.scenario: A participant is active, MFA status is enrolled, the record keeper is RK: LT Trust, and the plan profile shows loans are available.
examples.outcome: Guide the participant using the LT Trust article path and the ForUsAll login route, because the internal checks support using the record-keeper-specific online guidance.

examples.scenario: A participant is active, the record keeper is RK: Vanguard, and the plan offers loans.
examples.outcome: Guide the participant using the Vanguard article path and the Vanguard login route, because the participant matches the Vanguard-specific routing criteria.

examples.scenario: A participant is active and the plan offers loans, but the record keeper is neither RK: LT Trust nor RK: Vanguard.
examples.outcome: Do not route the participant to the LT Trust or Vanguard-specific articles. Continue with the remaining general loan guidance based on confirmed profile eligibility criteria and the article content, then direct record-keeper-specific help to support.

examples.scenario: A participant asks how to request a loan, but their profile does not support Active Participant Status.
examples.outcome: Do not guide the participant through the standard loan-request path described for active employees. Explain that the internal status check does not support proceeding through that route.

examples.scenario: A participant says they want a loan, but the plan profile does not show that loans are available.
examples.outcome: Do not tell the participant they can request a loan. Explain that loan availability depends on the employer's plan rules and must be confirmed before proceeding.

examples.scenario: A participant cannot access the account and wants to know whether loans are available under the plan.
examples.outcome: Use internal profile checks to confirm whether the plan offers loans. If participant access is unavailable, provide support help instead of relying only on self-service guidance.

examples.scenario: A participant has an existing unpaid loan with an outstanding balance and asks whether they can take another loan.
examples.outcome: Review current loan records and the plan's loan-limit fields before giving a conclusion, because an existing active unpaid loan may affect how many new loans can be requested.

examples.scenario: A participant sees a pending loan request in the dashboard but does not know whether the money was sent by check, wire, or ACH.
examples.outcome: Direct the participant to review the Loans, Distributions, or Transaction History section for the disbursement method and amount, then use that information to explain the next steps.

examples.scenario: A participant submitted a loan request yesterday and wants to know why the funds have not arrived yet.
examples.outcome: Explain that loan processing usually takes about 1 to 2 business days, although actual timing may vary depending on the provider and the delivery method selected.

examples.scenario: A participant reports that a check was issued for the loan, but only one week has passed and the check has not arrived.
examples.outcome: Explain that the article supports requesting a stop payment and reissue only if more than two weeks have passed since the check was issued.

examples.scenario: A participant reports that more than two weeks have passed since the check issue date and the funds still have not arrived.
examples.outcome: Explain that a stop payment and reissue may be requested and that the participant will need to confirm the correct mailing address.

examples.scenario: A participant's ACH transfer failed because the bank account details were entered incorrectly.
examples.outcome: Explain that failed ACH or wire transfers are typically automatically reissued by check to the address on file.

examples.scenario: A participant wants another reissue immediately after a failed ACH was reissued by check yesterday.
examples.outcome: Explain that the participant is asked to wait two full weeks from the check issue date before requesting another reissue.

examples.scenario: A participant wants to round the payroll deduction from $91.09 to $100 per paycheck to pay the loan off faster.
examples.outcome: Explain that the participant cannot choose an arbitrary amount and must use an exact multiple of the original scheduled payment.

examples.scenario: A participant wants to pay extra toward the loan by sending a small ACH payment that does not cover the full remaining balance.
examples.outcome: Explain that partial external payments by check, ACH, or wire are not accepted unless they cover the full remaining balance.

examples.scenario: A participant wants to pay the entire remaining loan balance before the scheduled end date.
examples.outcome: Explain that a full early payoff is allowed and that there is no prepayment penalty for paying the loan off in full.

examples.scenario: An employer changes 401(k) providers while the participant still has an outstanding loan and remains actively employed.
examples.outcome: Explain that the loan will usually transfer with the participant's account, the repayment schedule generally stays the same, and payroll deductions usually continue after the transition is complete.

examples.scenario: A participant does not see the outstanding loan immediately after the plan transitions to a new provider.
examples.outcome: Explain that there may be a short blackout period and a brief delay before the loan appears on the new provider's dashboard. If it still does not appear after a few business days, the participant should contact the new provider or custodian, and ForUsAll can also help verify the transfer.

examples.scenario: A participant leaves employment with an outstanding loan and does not repay the balance within the typical repayment window.
examples.outcome: Explain that the unpaid balance is generally treated as a loan offset or deemed distribution, becomes taxable income, and is reported on Form 1099-R. If the participant is under age 59 1/2, a 10% early withdrawal penalty may also apply.

examples.scenario: An employer terminates the 401(k) plan while the participant still has an outstanding loan balance.
examples.outcome: Explain that payroll loan repayments stop, the remaining balance is generally due in full before the plan closes, and if it is not repaid, it is treated as a taxable distribution. Also explain that online account access is disabled once the plan is fully terminated and distributed.

notes: No credit check is required for a 401(k) loan under this article's guidance.
The general maximum loan amount is typically 50% of the vested account balance up to $50,000.
That general cap may be reduced by the participant's highest outstanding loan balance during the previous 12 months, even if the earlier loan has already been repaid.
If the available data does not include enough 12-month outstanding loan history to confirm that reduction, the exact maximum loan amount should not be confirmed by the bot.
When the prior 12-month loan-balance reduction cannot be verified, the participant should be referred to human support.
After submitting a loan request, processing usually takes about 1 to 2 business days.
Actual timing may vary depending on the provider and the delivery method selected.
If the loan status is unclear, the participant can review the dashboard for pending or completed activity, including disbursement method and amount.
If loan funds were sent by check and more than two weeks have passed since the check was issued, a stop payment and reissue may be requested.
If a failed wire or ACH transfer is automatically reissued by check, the participant should wait two full weeks from the check issue date before requesting another reissue.
There may be a short blackout period during a provider change when account access and loan activity are temporarily unavailable.
After a provider transition, there may be a brief delay before the loan appears on the new provider's dashboard.
A loan is not treated as a taxable distribution as long as it is repaid according to the rules.
If employment ends while a loan is still outstanding and the balance is not repaid in time, it is generally treated as a loan offset or deemed distribution and becomes taxable income.
If the employer terminates the 401(k) plan and the remaining loan balance is not repaid before the plan closes, it is generally treated as a taxable distribution.
Taxable unpaid loan amounts are generally reported on Form 1099-R.
If the participant is under age 59 1/2, a 10% early withdrawal penalty may also apply.

A participant may avoid taxes and penalties after a loan offset by rolling over an amount equal to the offset into an IRA or another eligible retirement plan by the federal tax return due date for the year of the offset, including extensions.

faq_pairs.question: Can a loan fee be disclosed before the Record Keeper is confirmed?
faq_pairs.answer: No. The Record Keeper must be identified first before any loan-related fee is disclosed.

faq_pairs.question: What should happen if the Record Keeper cannot be determined and the participant asks about fees?
faq_pairs.answer: The participant should be redirected to support for fee confirmation before any loan-related fee is disclosed.

faq_pairs.question: Does the same loan fee apply to every plan?
faq_pairs.answer: No. Fees may vary depending on the Record Keeper or custodian and should not be generalized across all plans.

faq_pairs.question: Is there a loan fee for RK: Vanguard plans?
faq_pairs.answer: Yes. For RK: Vanguard plans, there is a one-time loan fee of $150 that is taken out of the loan proceeds.

faq_pairs.question: How is the Vanguard loan fee charged?
faq_pairs.answer: For RK: Vanguard plans, the one-time $150 loan fee is taken out of the loan proceeds.

faq_pairs.question: Can the bot disclose LT Trust loan fees before confirming the Record Keeper?
faq_pairs.answer: No. LT Trust fee rules should only be disclosed after the Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: LT Trust.

faq_pairs.question: What should the bot do if the participant's Record Keeper is neither RK: Vanguard nor RK: LT Trust and they ask about fees?
faq_pairs.answer: The bot should explain that support must confirm any loan-related fees first for non-Vanguard and non-LT Trust plans.

faq_pairs.question: Is there an additional fee for wire delivery on LT Trust plans?
faq_pairs.answer: Yes. For LT Trust plans, an additional $35 wire fee applies if wire is selected, and it applies per wire, not per request.

faq_pairs.question: Is there an additional fee for Overnight Check on LT Trust plans?
faq_pairs.answer: Yes. For LT Trust plans, an additional $50 Overnight Check fee applies, and it applies per overnight check, not per request.

faq_pairs.question: Are LT Trust wire and Overnight Check fees refundable?
faq_pairs.answer: No. For LT Trust plans, wire transfer and Overnight Check fees are non-refundable.

faq_pairs.question: Can the exact maximum loan amount always be confirmed automatically?
faq_pairs.answer: Not always. Although the general rule is 50% of the vested account balance up to $50,000, that cap may be reduced by the participant's highest outstanding loan balance during the previous 12 months, even if that earlier loan has already been repaid. If that 12-month loan history cannot be confirmed from the available data, only the general rule should be shared and the participant should be referred to human support for confirmation of the exact maximum.

faq_pairs.question: Does every 401(k) plan offer loans?
faq_pairs.answer: No. Not every retirement plan offers loans, and eligibility depends on the employer's plan rules.

faq_pairs.question: How can a participant check whether their plan allows loans?
faq_pairs.answer: The participant can review the Loans & Distributions section in the portal, but the workflow should first confirm loan availability through internal profile checks such as Maximum Number of Loans and related plan fields.

faq_pairs.question: What is the general maximum amount a participant can borrow?
faq_pairs.answer: The general maximum is 50% of the participant's vested account balance, up to $50,000, subject to reduction by the highest outstanding loan balance during the previous 12 months.

faq_pairs.question: How long does repayment usually last?
faq_pairs.answer: The standard repayment term is usually up to 5 years. If the loan is used to purchase a primary residence, the term may extend up to 15 years with supporting documentation.

faq_pairs.question: How are most loans repaid?
faq_pairs.answer: Most 401(k) loans are repaid automatically through payroll deductions.

faq_pairs.question: What should a participant do if they selected the wrong loan purpose?
faq_pairs.answer: They should contact support so the request can be rejected and resubmitted correctly.

faq_pairs.question: How can a participant check loan status after submitting a request?
faq_pairs.answer: They should log in to the plan dashboard and review the Loans, Distributions, or Transaction History section for pending or completed activity, including the disbursement method and amount.

faq_pairs.question: What happens if loan funds do not arrive?
faq_pairs.answer: The answer depends on the delivery method. If funds were sent by check and more than two weeks have passed since issue, a stop payment and reissue may be requested. If the original method was wire or ACH and the transfer failed, the funds are typically automatically reissued by check to the address on file.

faq_pairs.question: Can a participant make partial external payments to reduce the balance faster?
faq_pairs.answer: No. Partial external payments by check, ACH, or wire are not accepted unless they cover the full remaining balance.

faq_pairs.question: Is there a prepayment penalty for paying off the loan in full?
faq_pairs.answer: No. There is no prepayment penalty for paying the loan off in full.

faq_pairs.question: What happens to an outstanding loan if the employer changes 401(k) providers?
faq_pairs.answer: It will usually transfer with the participant's account as long as the participant is still actively employed. The remaining balance and repayment schedule generally stay the same, and payroll deductions continue after the transition is complete.

faq_pairs.question: What happens if the participant leaves employment with an outstanding loan?
faq_pairs.answer: The participant will typically have about 90 days to repay the remaining balance. If not repaid in time, the amount is generally treated as a taxable loan offset or deemed distribution and reported on Form 1099-R.

faq_pairs.question: Does a participant need a credit check to take a 401(k) loan?
faq_pairs.answer: No. The article states that no credit check is required for a 401(k) loan.

faq_pairs.question: Is a 401(k) loan treated as a taxable distribution when it is first issued?
faq_pairs.answer: No. A 401(k) loan is not treated as a taxable distribution as long as it is repaid according to the rules.

faq_pairs.question: Can a participant borrow the full $50,000 maximum in every case?
faq_pairs.answer: No. The general limit is 50% of the participant's vested account balance, up to $50,000, and that cap may also be reduced by the highest outstanding loan balance during the previous 12 months.

faq_pairs.question: Can a plan require a minimum loan amount?
faq_pairs.answer: Yes. The article states that a plan may require a minimum loan amount, such as $1,000.

faq_pairs.question: What documentation may be required for a primary residence loan?
faq_pairs.answer: Supporting documentation may be required, such as a signed purchase agreement or a loan estimate.

faq_pairs.question: How can a participant see how the loan proceeds were sent?
faq_pairs.answer: The participant can review the plan dashboard under Loans, Distributions, or Transaction History for pending or completed activity, including the disbursement method and amount.

faq_pairs.question: What if a participant wants to switch from a replacement check to ACH?
faq_pairs.answer: Support can send updated authorization forms if the participant wants to switch from a replacement check to ACH.

faq_pairs.question: Can a participant choose any larger payroll deduction amount to repay the loan faster?
faq_pairs.answer: No. Increased payroll deductions must be exact multiples of the original scheduled payment. Arbitrary amounts are not allowed.

faq_pairs.question: Does increasing payroll deductions change the loan's amortization or interest calculation?
faq_pairs.answer: No. The repayment schedule is not reamortized, so the loan terms and interest calculation do not change, although the loan will be paid off sooner if higher payments continue.

faq_pairs.question: What happens to online account access if the employer terminates the 401(k) plan?
faq_pairs.answer: Once the plan is fully terminated and distributed, online account access is disabled. Participants should make sure their mailing address is current so they can receive tax forms and instructions.

definitions.definition: A loan that lets a participant borrow money from the retirement account and repay it over time, usually through payroll deductions. The interest paid goes back into the participant's own 401(k). No credit check is required under this article's guidance.
The vested portion of the participant's account balance used to determine general loan availability and loan amount limits described in the article.
A 401(k) loan used to purchase a primary residence. The repayment term may extend up to 15 years if the required supporting documentation is provided.
Automatic loan repayments taken from the participant's pay and used to repay the 401(k) loan over time.
The unpaid amount remaining on a participant's loan. Any Outstanding Balance greater than $0.00 should be treated as active or unpaid; $0.00 should be treated as not active or unpaid.
The treatment of an unpaid outstanding loan balance as a distribution for tax purposes when repayment is not completed on time after job separation or plan termination.
A tax treatment applied to an unpaid loan balance when the loan is not repaid according to the rules described in the article.
An amount treated as taxable income for tax purposes. Under the article, an unpaid loan balance may become a taxable distribution if it is not repaid as required after certain events.
The tax form used to report the taxable amount of a loan offset or other distribution described in the article.
A deposit of an amount equal to the loan offset into an IRA or another eligible retirement plan to avoid taxation, if completed by the applicable federal tax return due date, including extensions.
The way loan proceeds are delivered to the participant, such as check, wire, or ACH transfer.
A process that may be requested when a check was issued but has not arrived after more than two weeks, so the original check can be stopped and a replacement issued.
A short period during a provider change when account access and loan activity may be temporarily unavailable.
The provider associated with the participant's plan that determines which login path, article guidance, fee rules, or loan-request route should be used in the workflow.
The participant's multi-factor authentication enrollment status, used in the workflow only to determine whether the LT Trust online account path may be available.
Automated Clearing House, an electronic funds transfer system used to send or receive loan proceeds or repayment amounts between bank accounts.
Individual Retirement Account, an eligible retirement account into which a participant may roll over a loan offset amount to avoid taxes and penalties.

The recalculation of a loan's repayment schedule to reflect changes in the payment amount or term. Under this article's guidance, 401(k) loans are not reamortized when higher payroll deductions are used.
An electronic signature tool that may be used as an alternate submission path for loan requests when online portal access is unavailable, depending on the record keeper's workflow.

must_not: Do not guarantee that a participant's plan allows loans.
Do not assert eligibility requirements beyond active employment, enough vested balance, IRS loan requirements, and plan-specific rules stated in the article.
Do not provide any processing or delivery timeline other than the timing stated in the article.
Do not say a participant can increase payroll deductions by any arbitrary amount.
Do not say partial external payments are accepted unless they cover the full remaining balance.
Do not guarantee that a loan transfer during a provider change is immediate or visible without delay.
Do not guarantee that taxes or penalties will be avoided unless the participant completes the rollover described in the article within the applicable deadline.
Do not imply online account access remains available after the plan is fully terminated and distributed.
Do not tell a participant they can request a loan until plan loan availability has been confirmed.
Do not treat MFA enrollment by itself as proof that the participant is eligible for a loan.
Do not assume the same login website or article path applies to every record keeper.
Do not route a participant to the LT Trust or Vanguard article path unless the record keeper matches that route.
Do not promise that a participant can submit a loan online unless the participant's profile and record-keeper-specific route support that path.
Do not tell a participant that the loan purpose can simply be edited after submission.
Do not confirm the primary residence repayment term without the required supporting documentation.
Do not state that a participant can ignore an unpaid loan after job separation or plan termination without tax consequences.
Do not say a failed wire or ACH will be corrected using any delivery method other than the check reissue outcome described in the article.
Do not allow another check reissue request before the article's stated two-week waiting period has passed when funds were reissued by check.
Do not say a participant must reapply for an existing loan after a provider change unless the applicable guidance explicitly says so.
Do not claim that a participant will always avoid taxes and penalties after a loan offset without mentioning the rollover deadline requirements.

Do not ask the participant for internal profile data that should be checked in internal systems, such as participant status, MFA status, record keeper, or active loan profile details, when the workflow expects the agent to verify those fields internally.
Do not share record-keeper-specific login links or article guidance before confirming the participant's record keeper.
Do not state that all participants can take multiple loans at once; concurrent loan limits may depend on the plan.
Do not convert operational assumptions into article facts when the article only states general rules or plan variation.
Do not disclose any loan-related fee before the participant's Record Keeper is confirmed.
Do not assume the same loan fee applies to all plans or all Record Keepers.
Do not apply Vanguard fee rules unless the participant's Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: Vanguard.
Do not apply LT Trust fee rules unless the participant's Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: LT Trust.
Do not disclose or estimate a loan-related fee for a non-Vanguard and non-LT Trust plan without support confirmation.
Do not generalize Vanguard or LT Trust fee rules to other Record Keepers.
Do not present a loan-related fee as guaranteed when the Record Keeper is unknown or the fee rule is not confirmed.
Do not state that a participant has no loan-related fee unless the applicable Record Keeper fee rule has been confirmed.
Do not disclose LT Trust delivery fees, maintenance fees, or processing fees unless the participant's Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: LT Trust.
Do not disclose the Vanguard $150 loan fee unless the participant's Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: Vanguard.
Do not treat support confirmation as optional when the participant's Record Keeper is neither RK: Vanguard nor RK: LT Trust and a fee question is being asked.
Do not guess, infer, or improvise a fee amount when fee details cannot be confirmed from the applicable Record Keeper rules.
must_do_if_unsure: Confirm the participant's status internally before guiding them through a loan-request path.
Confirm MFA status internally before relying on an LT Trust online account route.
Confirm the participant's record keeper before sharing a login website or record-keeper-specific article.
Confirm that the plan offers loans before explaining how the participant can request one.
Review current loan records and outstanding balances before discussing whether another loan may be available.
Use only the general guidance in this article when the participant's record keeper is neither LT Trust nor Vanguard unless another matching record-keeper-specific article is confirmed.
Direct the participant to support when account access, loan status, delivery status, or request routing remains unclear after the available checks.
Keep the explanation grounded in the participant's confirmed profile data and the article content when plan rules or routing are uncertain.
If the prior 12-month highest outstanding loan balance cannot be verified, explain the general loan-limit rule and refer the participant to human support for the exact maximum amount.
If available data is not sufficient to confirm the prior 12-month loan-balance reduction, do not estimate the participant's exact borrowing limit.

When a participant asks for the exact maximum loan amount and the prior 12-month highest outstanding balance is unavailable, share only the general rule and escalate the exact calculation to human support.
Confirm the participant's Record Keeper before disclosing any loan-related fee.
If the Record Keeper cannot be determined, redirect the participant to support for fee confirmation.
If the Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: Vanguard, disclose only the Vanguard fee rules.
If the Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: LT Trust, disclose only the LT Trust fee rules that are confirmed in scope.
If the Record Keeper is neither RK: Vanguard nor RK: LT Trust, direct the participant to support before disclosing any loan-related fee.
When a participant asks about fees and the applicable Record Keeper fee rule is not confirmed, explain that fees may vary by Record Keeper or custodian and avoid quoting an amount.
Use only the fee rules tied to the participant's confirmed Record Keeper, and do not generalize those fees to other plans.
If fee details remain unclear after available checks, provide only the support-routing outcome rather than a fee estimate.

eligibility_requirements: The participant's plan must offer loans.
The participant must be actively employed.
The participant must have enough vested balance available to satisfy the applicable loan requirements.
The participant must meet IRS loan requirements.
The participant's Participant Status must support the active-employee loan-request path used in the workflow.
The participant's profile must be correctly matched before any loan eligibility or routing conclusion is given.
The participant's Record Keeper must be confirmed before the correct submission route, article path, or fee disclosure is selected.
If the participant is being guided through an online account route for LT Trust handling, MFA status must support that route before online submission guidance is given.
The participant's plan-level Maximum Number of Loans must allow another loan based on the current plan limit.
The participant must not already have active unpaid loans that meet or exceed the plan's concurrent-loan limit.
The participant's current loan history must be reviewed using Outstanding Balance values to determine whether any listed loans are still active and unpaid.
If the participant is requesting a primary residence loan term, the required supporting documentation must be available before the longer repayment term is treated as applicable.

If the participant is asking for the exact maximum loan amount, the prior 12-month highest outstanding loan balance must be verifiable; otherwise the exact maximum cannot be confirmed through the bot flow.
If the participant is asking about any loan-related fee, the Record Keeper must be confirmed before any fee is disclosed.

The participant's loan route must align with the confirmed Record Keeper: LT Trust and Vanguard may follow their respective guided paths, while other Record Keepers receive only general guidance plus support handling.
blocking_conditions: The participant's plan does not offer loans.
The participant is not actively employed when active employment is required for the requested outcome.
The participant's Participant Status does not support the active-employee loan-request path used in the workflow.
The participant does not have enough vested balance available to satisfy the applicable loan requirements.
The participant's plan-level Maximum Number of Loans does not allow another active loan.

allowed_conclusions: The participant may be able to take a 401(k) loan if the plan allows loans and the general conditions in the article are met.
The general maximum loan amount is 50% of vested balance up to $50,000, subject to the prior-12-month outstanding-loan reduction described in the article.
Most loans are repaid through payroll deductions and usually have a term of up to 5 years.
A primary residence loan may have a term of up to 15 years if supporting documentation is provided.
If the participant leaves employment with an outstanding loan and does not repay it on time, the unpaid amount is generally treated as taxable income and reported on Form 1099-R.
A participant may avoid taxes and penalties after a loan offset by completing the rollover described in the article by the applicable federal tax return deadline, including extensions.
The bot may explain the general loan limit rule, but should not confirm the exact maximum amount when the prior 12-month highest outstanding loan balance cannot be verified.
If prior 12-month loan history is unavailable, the participant may be referred to human support for confirmation of the exact borrowing limit.
The bot may state that the general maximum is typically 50% of vested balance up to $50,000, while clarifying that the final amount may be reduced by prior 12-month loan history.
If the participant's Record Keeper is RK: LT Trust and the required workflow checks support the online path, the participant may be guided using the LT Trust article and ForUsAll login route.
If the participant's Record Keeper is RK: Vanguard and the required workflow checks support that path, the participant may be guided using the Vanguard article and Vanguard login route.

If the participant's Record Keeper is neither RK: LT Trust nor RK: Vanguard, the participant may be given only the general loan guidance from this article and redirected to support for record-keeper-specific assistance.
MFA status may be used only to determine whether the LT Trust online account route is available, but not as a standalone loan-eligibility conclusion.
If the participant's plan does not offer loans, the bot may conclude that the participant cannot proceed with a loan request through this workflow.
If the participant selected the wrong loan purpose, the bot may conclude that the request should be rejected and resubmitted correctly through support.
If the participant wants to repay the loan faster, the bot may conclude that payroll deductions may be increased only in exact multiples of the original scheduled payment.
If the participant wants to pay off the loan early, the bot may conclude that a full payoff is allowed and that there is no prepayment penalty for paying the loan off in full.
If loan funds were sent by check and more than two weeks have passed since the check was issued, the bot may conclude that a stop payment and reissue may be requested.

If a wire or ACH transfer failed because of incorrect banking details, name mismatch, or account type issues, the bot may conclude that the funds are typically automatically reissued by check to the address on file.

If an employer changes 401(k) providers while the participant remains actively employed, the bot may conclude that the loan will usually transfer with the account and that payroll deductions generally continue after the transition is complete.

If the loan does not appear immediately after a provider transition, the bot may conclude that there may be a short blackout period or a brief delay before the loan appears on the new provider's dashboard.
If the participant leaves employment with an outstanding loan, the bot may conclude that the participant will typically have about 90 days to repay the remaining balance.
If the employer terminates the 401(k) plan, the bot may conclude that payroll loan repayments stop and the remaining balance is generally due in full before the plan closes.

If the plan is fully terminated and distributed, the bot may conclude that online account access is disabled and that the participant should make sure the mailing address on file is current for tax forms and instructions.
If the participant's Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: Vanguard, the bot may conclude that there is a one-time loan fee of $150 taken out of the loan proceeds.

If the participant asks about loan-related fees and the Record Keeper cannot be determined, the bot may conclude that fee disclosure cannot be completed and that the participant should be redirected to support.
If the participant's Record Keeper is neither RK: Vanguard nor RK: LT Trust, the bot may conclude that support must confirm any loan-related fees before they are disclosed.
If the participant's Record Keeper is confirmed as RK: LT Trust, the bot may disclose only the LT Trust fee rules that are confirmed in scope for that request.
If the participant asks about fees, the bot may conclude that fee disclosure depends on the confirmed Record Keeper and should not be generalized across all plans.
